narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, we know how much heart and determination it takes to pursue this path, and we're here to help you understand the automated decision on your application in a clear way. Your profile includes Year 1 grades placing you in the 3rd decile at your school—below the typical 6th decile for applicants—Year 3 grades in the 2nd decile, also lower than the average 6th decile, an undergraduate GPA of 3.0 compared to the usual 3.21, an LSAT score of 29 which is notably below the average of 37, and a lower-middle family income bracket. You're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for most applicants. The model's assessment gave you a 52.6% predicted chance of failing the bar exam on your first try, tipping the scales just enough to reject your application, as the school prioritizes those predicted to pass with high confidence. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, which stood out as a strong signal of potential challenges ahead compared to peers. Your Year 1 decile also contributed notably in that direction, reinforcing concerns about consistent academic performance relative to typical students. On a more positive note, your lower-middle family income actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing more than average circumstances might. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA and LSAT score helped nudge things toward success, though not enough to overcome the top concerns, while your full-time status had a minor positive influence. Overall, your grades in later years carried the most weight here, setting you apart from applicants who typically rank higher in school deciles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and exploring scholarships or support networks tailored for Black women in law to address income-related barriers. You're capable of turning this around—many do—and we encourage reapplying after addressing these key areas. Keep pushing; your journey matters.
